prompt:
Before answering, invoke /meta:socratic and work through the protocol.
Consider what type of thinking this problem actually requires before jumping
to a solution.


Before solving this problem, analyze it:
(a) What type of problem is this?
(b) What cognitive strategy is most relevant?
(c) What will the hardest part be?
Then solve it.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"problem_type": "...", "strategy": "...", "hardest_part": "...", "solution": "..."}

Problem: A software company is losing 5% of users per month (churn). The product
team proposes building 10 new features that exiting users requested
in surveys. The data team notes that users who leave typically used
only 2 of the existing 30 features, while users who stay use an
average of 8 features. What does this suggest the real problem is,
and what would be a more effective intervention than building new
features?

parsed.problem_type: Causal inference / attribution error under survivorship and survey bias
parsed.strategy: Steelman then falsify: construct the strongest case FOR the product team's proposal, then check whether the data actually supports it — distinguishing correlation from causation in the churn signal
parsed.hardest_part: Resisting the intuitive move of trusting direct user feedback (survey data feels authoritative) while recognizing it may systematically misattribute cause. Churned users say 'I wanted feature X' but the behavioral data says 'you never used what was already there' — those tell different stories, and the behavioral data is harder to dismiss.
parsed.solution: The data pattern — churned users used 2/30 features, retained users used 8/30 — is the key signal. This is more consistent with an activation and engagement failure than a feature gap. Steelmanning the product team: churned users explicitly asked for features, so building them is listening to customers. But this steelman collapses under scrutiny: users who leave often misattribute cause (they say 'missing feature' when the real issue is they never got enough value from existing features to stay engaged long enough to need more). The 'used only 2 features' pattern is the fingerprint of users who churned before reaching the 'aha moment,' not users who exhausted the product. Adding 10 more features to a product where churning users already ignore 28 of 30 is unlikely to fix the underlying problem. A more effective intervention: (1) identify which feature combinations correlate with retention (what does 'uses 8 features' actually mean — is there a core cluster?), (2) redesign onboarding to drive new users to that cluster faster, (3) add in-product guidance and lifecycle nudges to push at-risk users (currently at 2 features) toward the retention threshold before they churn. The goal is not more features — it is getting users to genuinely adopt the ones that already deliver value.
